items.name: Amazon Athena
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Serverless SQL over S3; query governed data or evaluation results.
items.boundary: Eliminate if the scenario mandates transactional OLTP writes or low-latency ACID updates.
use_when: Querying structured or semi-structured data directly in Amazon S3 via SQL without managing databases or servers.
Generating ad-hoc analytical reports or evaluation queries directly from AWS Glue Data Catalog to Amazon QuickSight.
avoid_when: Eliminate if the scenario mandates transactional OLTP writes or low-latency ACID updates.
Eliminate if vector similarity search or hybrid semantic retrieval is required.
items.standout_feature: Zero-infrastructure serverless SQL querying over S3 data lakes and cataloged datasets using standard ANSI SQL.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Glue: Athena is for ad-hoc interactive SQL queries over S3 data, whereas Glue is for ETL data transformation and automated catalog crawlers.
vs Amazon OpenSearch Service: Athena is an ANSI SQL engine over S3 files, while OpenSearch is an indexed search engine built for fast full-text, log analytics, and vector search.
vs Amazon RDS: Athena queries raw files statelessly without persistent instance infrastructure, whereas RDS provides structured relational OLTP databases.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Serverless SQL over S3; query governed data or evaluation results. Not a transactional database.

items.name: Amazon EMR
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Managed big-data frameworks for very large custom Spark/Hadoop processing.
items.boundary: Eliminate if the scenario demands minimal operational overhead for standard ETL (prefer AWS Glue).
use_when: Executing vast petabyte-scale distributed data processing using custom Spark or Hadoop clusters.
Scenarios requiring deep control over open-source big data frameworks and compute cluster topology.
avoid_when: Eliminate if the scenario demands minimal operational overhead for standard ETL (prefer AWS Glue).
Eliminate if the task is a short, stateless, event-driven function (prefer AWS Lambda).
items.standout_feature: Managed big-data processing engine tailored for giant custom Apache Spark, Hadoop, and Hive workloads.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Glue: EMR offers custom cluster and framework configuration for complex Spark/Hadoop jobs, while Glue provides fully managed serverless ETL with zero cluster management.
vs SageMaker Processing: EMR is designed for general big-data cluster workloads, whereas SageMaker Processing is targeted specifically at ML data preprocessing and model evaluation containers.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Managed big-data frameworks for very large custom Spark/Hadoop processing. More operations than Glue for common managed ETL.

items.name: AWS Glue
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Serverless ETL, Data Catalog, crawlers, lineage integration, and Data Quality/DQDL.
items.boundary: Eliminate if interactive SQL querying over S3 is requested without transformation (prefer Amazon Athena).
use_when: Automated ETL pipelines, schema discovery via crawlers, and central cataloging in Glue Data Catalog.
Enforcing automated data quality checks and data transformation rules before model training or ingestion.
avoid_when: Eliminate if interactive SQL querying over S3 is requested without transformation (prefer Amazon Athena).
Eliminate if real-time low-latency event processing is required.
items.standout_feature: Serverless data integration and ETL platform with automated schema discovery crawlers and data quality governance (DQDL).
distinction_notes: vs Amazon Athena: Glue extracts, transforms, and catalogs data pipelines, whereas Athena queries cataloged S3 data statelessly via SQL.
vs SageMaker Data Wrangler: Glue is built for automated serverless batch ETL pipelines, while Data Wrangler is built for interactive ML data feature engineering.
vs Amazon EMR: Glue eliminates cluster lifecycle operations for standard ETL, while EMR requires framework or cluster customization.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Serverless ETL, Data Catalog, crawlers, lineage integration, and Data Quality/DQDL.

items.name: Amazon Kinesis
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Real-time streaming ingestion/processing.
items.boundary: Eliminate if the scenario requires simple asynchronous task queueing or backpressure (prefer SQS).
use_when: Continuously streaming real-time IoT, telemetry, or application log data into S3 or vector stores.
Scenarios requiring strictly ordered, replayable event streams at high ingestion rates.
avoid_when: Eliminate if the scenario requires simple asynchronous task queueing or backpressure (prefer SQS).
Eliminate if rule-based event routing across disparate services is required (prefer EventBridge).
items.standout_feature: Real-time streaming platform for high-throughput, ordered data ingestion and stream processing.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon SQS: Kinesis processes high-throughput ordered stream data read by multiple concurrent consumers, while SQS handles discrete message queues and task worker decoupling.
vs Amazon MSK: Kinesis is AWS-native managed streaming with simpler scaling, while MSK is managed open-source Apache Kafka.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Real-time streaming ingestion/processing. Use for ordered high-throughput streams, not simple task queues.

items.name: Amazon OpenSearch Service
items.importance_reason: Implementation-depth service used directly in production design and troubleshooting decisions.
items.role: Search, log analytics, vector/hybrid search, shards/index control.
items.boundary: Eliminate if minimal operational overhead RAG with fully managed indexing is specified (prefer Bedrock Knowledge Bases).
use_when: RAG workloads requiring hybrid search (combining sparse BM25 keyword matching with dense k-NN vector embeddings).
Operational log analytics, custom index lifecycle policy management, and precise vector index sharding.
avoid_when: Eliminate if minimal operational overhead RAG with fully managed indexing is specified (prefer Bedrock Knowledge Bases).
Eliminate if structured relational OLTP transactions are required.
items.standout_feature: Distributed search and vector engine providing hybrid keyword/semantic search, log analytics, and precise shard control.
distinction_notes: vs Bedrock Knowledge Bases: OpenSearch Service offers granular control over vector indexes, hybrid search weights, and cluster hardware, whereas Knowledge Bases is a fully managed RAG wrapper.
vs Amazon Kendra: OpenSearch Service is a general vector/search engine, whereas Kendra is an enterprise semantic search platform with pre-built connector security filters.
vs Amazon Aurora: OpenSearch is optimized for similarity search and unstructured retrieval, whereas Aurora is for relational ACID database transactions.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Search, log analytics, vector/hybrid search, shards/index control.

items.name: Amazon Quick Sight (formerly Amazon QuickSight)
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Dashboards, evaluation reporting, analytics, and stakeholder visualization within the current Amazon Quick family.
items.boundary: Eliminate if the requirement is live raw log aggregation or metric alerting (prefer CloudWatch or Grafana).
use_when: Visualizing GenAI evaluation outputs, model latency metrics, or business analytics in interactive dashboards.
Embedding natural-language Q&A and visual analytics into internal enterprise web applications.
avoid_when: Eliminate if the requirement is live raw log aggregation or metric alerting (prefer CloudWatch or Grafana).
Eliminate if data extraction or transformation is required.
items.standout_feature: Cloud-native business intelligence and visualization service for building dashboards and AI model evaluation reporting.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon Managed Grafana: QuickSight is for business dashboarding and executive evaluation reporting, whereas Grafana is for operational metrics and infrastructure monitoring.
vs Amazon Q Business: QuickSight presents charts and visual data analytics, whereas Q Business provides a conversational AI assistant answering enterprise document queries.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Dashboards, evaluation reporting, analytics, and stakeholder visualization within the current Amazon Quick family.

items.name: Amazon MSK
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Managed Apache Kafka for existing Kafka/event-streaming requirements; heavier than native queues/events.
items.boundary: Eliminate if lowest operational overhead AWS-native queueing or streaming is desired without Kafka requirements.
use_when: Migrating or integrating existing open-source Apache Kafka applications to AWS.
High-throughput enterprise event streaming where Kafka API compatibility is strictly required.
avoid_when: Eliminate if lowest operational overhead AWS-native queueing or streaming is desired without Kafka requirements.
Eliminate if simple point-to-point task queueing is needed (prefer SQS).
items.standout_feature: Fully managed Apache Kafka service for running existing Kafka event streaming applications seamlessly on AWS.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon Kinesis: MSK runs standard open-source Apache Kafka APIs, whereas Kinesis is an AWS-native managed streaming service.
vs Amazon EventBridge: MSK streams continuous high-throughput event logs, whereas EventBridge routes discrete events based on pattern matching rules.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Managed Apache Kafka for existing Kafka/event-streaming requirements; heavier than native queues/events.

items.name: Amazon AppFlow
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Managed SaaS-to-AWS data transfer, such as Salesforce content to S3.
items.boundary: Eliminate if custom API backend development or microservice routing is required (prefer API Gateway).
use_when: Ingesting SaaS data from Salesforce, Slack, or ServiceNow into Amazon S3 or vector stores for RAG.
Automated SaaS data ingestion pipelines operating with PrivateLink encryption and low setup complexity.
avoid_when: Eliminate if custom API backend development or microservice routing is required (prefer API Gateway).
Eliminate if database-to-database migration is requested (prefer AWS DMS).
items.standout_feature: No-code data integration service for securely transferring data between SaaS applications (e.g. Salesforce, ServiceNow) and AWS.
distinction_notes: vs AWS DataSync: AppFlow integrates SaaS software products (Salesforce, Zendesk), whereas DataSync migrates storage file systems (NFS, SMB, S3).
vs Amazon API Gateway: AppFlow is a point-to-click SaaS ingestion tool, whereas API Gateway publishes and manages custom REST/HTTP APIs.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Managed SaaS-to-AWS data transfer, such as Salesforce content to S3.

items.name: AWS AppConfig
items.importance_reason: Implementation-depth service used directly in production design and troubleshooting decisions.
items.role: Validated runtime configuration, feature flags, model/routing changes, staged deployment, and rollback.
items.boundary: Eliminate if storing persistent database records or relational data.
use_when: Dynamically switching foundation model endpoints or RAG thresholds without redeploying application code.
Implementing feature flags, canary rollouts, and automated configuration rollback upon alarm triggers.
avoid_when: Eliminate if storing persistent database records or relational data.
Eliminate if storing high-security API secrets or database passwords (prefer Secrets Manager).
items.standout_feature: Runtime configuration deployment service for dynamic feature flags, model routing updates, and safe staged rollouts.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Secrets Manager: AppConfig manages application runtime configuration and feature flags, while Secrets Manager stores and rotates confidential credentials.
vs AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store: AppConfig provides dynamic staged deployment and alarm monitoring for configurations, whereas Parameter Store is a static key-value parameter repository.
vs Bedrock Prompt Management: AppConfig manages runtime feature toggles and routing rules, while Bedrock Prompt Management versions and tests prompt templates.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Validated runtime configuration, feature flags, model/routing changes, staged deployment, and rollback.

items.name: Amazon EventBridge
items.importance_reason: Implementation-depth service used directly in production design and troubleshooting decisions.
items.role: Rule-based event routing and fanout to changing consumers.
items.boundary: Eliminate if durable task queueing, batch worker polling, or backpressure management is required (prefer SQS).
use_when: Decoupling asynchronous microservices via event pattern rules and multi-target fanout.
Triggering GenAI processing workflows in response to S3 object creation or system state changes.
avoid_when: Eliminate if durable task queueing, batch worker polling, or backpressure management is required (prefer SQS).
Eliminate if complex multi-step workflow orchestration with state history is required (prefer Step Functions).
items.standout_feature: Serverless event bus for decoupled, rule-based event routing and fanout across microservices and AWS services.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon SQS: EventBridge routes events based on rules to targets without queue polling, while SQS holds durable work queues for consumer polling and backpressure.
vs AWS Step Functions: EventBridge triggers decoupled stateless reactions to events, while Step Functions orchestrates stateful, multi-step execution workflows.
vs Amazon SNS: EventBridge provides advanced JSON payload filtering and broad AWS service integration, whereas SNS focuses on high-throughput pub/sub push notifications.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Rule-based event routing and fanout to changing consumers. Not a work queue/backpressure layer.

items.name: Amazon SNS
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Push fanout and notifications, including human-review alerts.
items.boundary: Eliminate if message queuing, polling, or backpressure buffering is required (prefer SQS).
use_when: Pushing urgent alerts or human-in-the-loop review notifications simultaneously to email, SMS, and webhooks.
Fanout pattern delivering identical event notifications to multiple SQS queues in parallel.
avoid_when: Eliminate if message queuing, polling, or backpressure buffering is required (prefer SQS).
Eliminate if complex event payload filtering across multiple JSON schema attributes is needed (prefer EventBridge).
items.standout_feature: High-throughput publish/subscribe notification service for instant message push fanout to HTTP, SMS, Email, and Lambda.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon SQS: SNS pushes messages instantly to subscribers (pub/sub), while SQS requires consumers to pull messages from a queue (queueing/polling).
vs Amazon EventBridge: SNS is optimized for mass push notifications and SQS fanout, while EventBridge provides rich rule-based schema routing.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Push fanout and notifications, including human-review alerts.

items.name: Amazon SQS
items.importance_reason: Implementation-depth service used directly in production design and troubleshooting decisions.
items.role: Durable queue, burst absorption, backpressure, asynchronous workers, dead-letter queues.
items.boundary: Eliminate if real-time continuous stream processing with log replay is required (prefer Kinesis or MSK).
use_when: Buffering unpredictable inference requests or document processing bursts to protect backend workers.
Decoupling producer services from worker pools using Dead Letter Queues (DLQ) for failed message handling.
avoid_when: Eliminate if real-time continuous stream processing with log replay is required (prefer Kinesis or MSK).
Eliminate if real-time pub/sub push delivery to webhooks without polling is specified (prefer SNS).
items.standout_feature: Durable serverless message queue service providing asynchronous worker decoupling, rate absorption, and backpressure control.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon SNS: SQS stores messages in a queue until pulled by workers (pull model), whereas SNS pushes messages immediately to subscribers (push model).
vs Amazon EventBridge: SQS buffers task requests to absorb traffic spikes and control worker concurrency, whereas EventBridge immediately routes events to targets.
vs AWS Step Functions: SQS manages isolated message task backlogs, whereas Step Functions manages step-by-step stateful workflow coordination.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Durable queue, burst absorption, backpressure, asynchronous workers, dead-letter queues.

items.name: AWS Step Functions
items.importance_reason: Implementation-depth service used directly in production design and troubleshooting decisions.
items.role: Deterministic orchestration, retries, branches, parallel work, long callbacks, agent loops, and audit history.
items.boundary: Eliminate if the requirement is simple prompt chaining inside a Bedrock graph (prefer Bedrock Prompt Flows).
use_when: Orchestrating multi-step GenAI pipelines requiring conditional branching, error handling, parallel steps, and retries.
Long-running workflows requiring human-in-the-loop callback tasks (e.g. waiting days for approval before model deployment).
avoid_when: Eliminate if the requirement is simple prompt chaining inside a Bedrock graph (prefer Bedrock Prompt Flows).
Eliminate if simple point-to-point task queueing is sufficient (prefer SQS).
items.standout_feature: Serverless visual workflow orchestrator for managing complex, stateful multi-step processes, branching, retries, and human approvals.
distinction_notes: vs Bedrock Prompt Flows: Step Functions provides general-purpose, enterprise stateful workflow orchestration across all AWS services, whereas Prompt Flows is optimized specifically for prompt-centric model execution graphs.
vs Amazon EventBridge: Step Functions coordinates stateful execution graphs with explicit state history, while EventBridge handles decoupled, stateless event routing.
vs AWS Lambda: Step Functions orchestrates flow control and state transitions between functions, while Lambda executes individual stateless code logic.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Deterministic orchestration, retries, branches, parallel work, long callbacks, agent loops, and audit history.

items.name: AWS App Runner
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Managed deployment of web/container services with low infrastructure ownership.
items.boundary: Eliminate if complex container orchestration across microservices is needed (prefer ECS or EKS).
use_when: Deploying containerized web applications or API microservices quickly without configuring ECS tasks or clusters.
Scenarios prioritizing lowest operational complexity for HTTP web services in container format.
avoid_when: Eliminate if complex container orchestration across microservices is needed (prefer ECS or EKS).
Eliminate if non-HTTP background worker processing is required.
items.standout_feature: Fully managed web service runner for deploying containerized web apps and APIs with zero infrastructure setup.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Fargate: App Runner automates container management, load balancing, and SSL setup for web apps out of the box, whereas Fargate requires explicit ECS task definitions and load balancer setup.
vs AWS Lambda: App Runner runs persistent container web servers, while Lambda runs short event-driven function executions.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Managed deployment of web/container services with low infrastructure ownership.

items.name: Amazon EC2
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Full VM control for workloads that do not fit managed/serverless choices; highest host operations.
items.boundary: Eliminate if scenario mandates minimal operational overhead or serverless compute (prefer Lambda or Fargate).
use_when: Running specialized ML frameworks or proprietary host software that cannot run on managed containers.
Custom hardware driver requirements or strict OS host access constraints.
avoid_when: Eliminate if scenario mandates minimal operational overhead or serverless compute (prefer Lambda or Fargate).
Eliminate if managed container management without server maintenance is requested.
items.standout_feature: Virtually customizable compute instances offering full root control over host operating systems, GPUs, and custom runtimes.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Fargate: EC2 requires full operating system patching and instance scaling management, whereas Fargate provides serverless compute for containers without server management.
vs AWS Lambda: EC2 hosts long-running dedicated instances, whereas Lambda executes stateless code on demand.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Full VM control for workloads that do not fit managed/serverless choices; highest host operations.

items.name: AWS Lambda
items.importance_reason: Implementation-depth service used directly in production design and troubleshooting decisions.
items.role: Stateless event/API/tool adapters, preprocessing, validators, and short orchestration tasks.
items.boundary: Eliminate if execution duration exceeds the 15-minute timeout limit.
use_when: Building custom API tool adapters for LLM agents, data preprocessing, or lightweight payload validation.
Executing short-duration event handlers triggered by S3 uploads, EventBridge events, or API Gateway calls.
avoid_when: Eliminate if execution duration exceeds the 15-minute timeout limit.
Eliminate if GPU hardware acceleration or long-running heavy background processing is required.
items.standout_feature: Serverless event-driven compute executing stateless functions in response to events with sub-second scaling.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Fargate: Lambda is designed for short (<15 min) event-driven functions, whereas Fargate hosts longer-running container tasks without execution time limits.
vs Amazon EC2: Lambda is serverless with zero host management, whereas EC2 requires provisioning and managing virtual machine hosts.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Stateless event/API/tool adapters, preprocessing, validators, and short orchestration tasks.

items.name: Lambda@Edge
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Run limited logic at CloudFront edge locations; not general FM inference.
items.boundary: Eliminate if general heavy foundation model inference or long database operations are needed.
use_when: Customizing HTTP headers, inspecting user authentication tokens, or URL rewriting at global CDN edge nodes.
Low-latency geographic routing or edge user payload validation before reaching backend origins.
avoid_when: Eliminate if general heavy foundation model inference or long database operations are needed.
Eliminate if non-HTTP streaming protocol processing is required.
items.standout_feature: Edge compute extension running Lambda function logic at CloudFront edge locations for ultra-low latency request customization.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Lambda: Lambda@Edge executes lightweight request/response transformations at global CloudFront edge locations, whereas standard Lambda runs inside regional VPCs/environments for backend logic.
vs AWS Wavelength: Lambda@Edge operates at CloudFront edge nodes for web traffic, whereas Wavelength operates inside 5G telecom networks for mobile edge compute.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Run limited logic at CloudFront edge locations; not general FM inference.

items.name: AWS Outposts
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: AWS infrastructure on premises for local preprocessing/data residency and hybrid access.
items.boundary: Eliminate if standard cloud Region deployment satisfies all security and compliance needs.
use_when: Strict local data residency constraints requiring compute and storage to physically remain inside an on-premises facility.
Ultra-low latency connectivity requirements between AWS services and legacy local industrial systems.
avoid_when: Eliminate if standard cloud Region deployment satisfies all security and compliance needs.
Eliminate if mobile 5G telecom edge latency is the explicit requirement (prefer Wavelength).
items.standout_feature: Fully managed service that extends AWS infrastructure, native services, and APIs directly to on-premises data centers.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Wavelength: Outposts installs physical AWS hardware in customer data centers for on-prem data residency, while Wavelength installs hardware in 5G carrier centers for mobile network edge latency.
vs AWS Local Zones: Outposts is physical hardware on customer premises, while Local Zones are AWS-managed infrastructure in specific metro areas.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: AWS infrastructure on premises for local preprocessing/data residency and hybrid access.

items.name: AWS Wavelength
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Ultra-low-latency compute at telecom edge; select only for explicit edge/mobile latency needs.
items.boundary: Eliminate if on-premises corporate data residency is the primary goal (prefer Outposts).
use_when: Delivering sub-10 millisecond latency applications to 5G mobile devices, AR/VR, or smart vehicles.
Real-time mobile inference requiring direct connection to telecommunication network edge nodes.
avoid_when: Eliminate if on-premises corporate data residency is the primary goal (prefer Outposts).
Eliminate if general web browser CDN caching is required (prefer CloudFront).
items.standout_feature: Infrastructure deployment embedding AWS compute and storage inside 5G telecom provider data centers for ultra-low-latency mobile apps.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Outposts: Wavelength targets 5G mobile edge latency inside telecom data centers, whereas Outposts targets customer on-premises data residency.
vs CloudFront / Lambda@Edge: Wavelength provides full compute instances inside 5G networks, whereas CloudFront/Lambda@Edge provides CDN content delivery and edge header manipulation.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Ultra-low-latency compute at telecom edge; select only for explicit edge/mobile latency needs.

items.name: Amazon ECR
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Store container images for ECS/EKS/SageMaker/custom runtimes.
items.boundary: Eliminate if container execution compute is required (ECR only stores container images).
use_when: Storing custom SageMaker training/inference container images or ECS/EKS microservice images.
Secure private container repository management integrated with IAM permissions and image vulnerability scanning.
avoid_when: Eliminate if container execution compute is required (ECR only stores container images).
Eliminate if storing general unstructured document files (prefer S3).
items.standout_feature: Fully managed container registry for storing, managing, and deploying Docker images and custom ML model containers.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon S3: ECR is specialized for Docker image layers and container OCI artifacts with native container runtime auth, whereas S3 is general object storage.
vs AWS CodeArtifact: ECR stores container images, while CodeArtifact stores application code package dependencies (npm, PyPI, Maven).
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Store container images for ECS/EKS/SageMaker/custom runtimes.

items.name: Amazon ECS
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: AWS-native container orchestration; pair with Fargate for managed compute.
items.boundary: Eliminate if existing enterprise Kubernetes tooling or APIs are required (prefer EKS).
use_when: Orchestrating containerized microservices or GenAI tools using AWS-native task definitions without Kubernetes complexity.
Deploying multi-container workloads on Fargate serverless compute or EC2 capacity.
avoid_when: Eliminate if existing enterprise Kubernetes tooling or APIs are required (prefer EKS).
Eliminate if simple event-driven code execution without containers is sufficient (prefer Lambda).
items.standout_feature: AWS-native container orchestration service providing simple, deeply integrated container cluster management.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon EKS: ECS uses simple AWS-native task definitions and concepts, whereas EKS provides open-source Kubernetes API compatibility.
vs AWS Fargate: ECS is the container orchestrator (control plane), whereas Fargate is the serverless compute engine (execution layer) that runs ECS tasks.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: AWS-native container orchestration; pair with Fargate for managed compute.

items.name: Amazon EKS
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Managed Kubernetes when Kubernetes is a requirement or existing platform, not the lowest-overhead default.
items.boundary: Eliminate if minimum operational overhead AWS-native deployment is requested (prefer ECS or App Runner).
use_when: Migrating existing Kubernetes clusters, Helm charts, or K8s operator tooling directly to AWS.
Mandated enterprise standardization on Kubernetes APIs for multi-cloud container orchestration.
avoid_when: Eliminate if minimum operational overhead AWS-native deployment is requested (prefer ECS or App Runner).
Eliminate if non-containerized serverless functions are sufficient (prefer Lambda).
items.standout_feature: Managed Kubernetes service for running containerized workloads with 100% open-source Kubernetes ecosystem compatibility.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon ECS: EKS requires Kubernetes API management and manifest complexity, whereas ECS offers a simpler AWS-native container model.
vs AWS Fargate: EKS is a Kubernetes orchestrator, while Fargate can be used as a serverless compute provider for EKS pods.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Managed Kubernetes when Kubernetes is a requirement or existing platform, not the lowest-overhead default.

items.name: AWS Fargate
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Serverless compute for ECS/EKS containers; useful for heavier/longer MCP tools.
items.boundary: Eliminate if direct host OS access, custom GPU passthrough, or specialized kernel configurations are needed (prefer EC2).
use_when: Deploying containerized microservices, long-running agent tools, or model serving containers with zero server management.
Scenarios requiring container isolation and flexible memory/vCPU allocation without cluster scaling operations.
avoid_when: Eliminate if direct host OS access, custom GPU passthrough, or specialized kernel configurations are needed (prefer EC2).
Eliminate if execution runs for short sub-second event bursts suitable for Lambda.
items.standout_feature: Serverless compute engine for Amazon ECS and EKS that runs containers without provisioning or managing EC2 servers.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon EC2: Fargate manages host infrastructure automatically for containers, whereas EC2 requires server provisioning, OS patching, and capacity planning.
vs AWS Lambda: Fargate has no execution duration limits and runs full container workloads, whereas Lambda has a 15-minute limit for stateless functions.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Serverless compute for ECS/EKS containers; useful for heavier/longer MCP tools.

items.name: Amazon Connect
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Cloud contact center; GenAI agent assist, call workflows, and transcripts.
items.boundary: Eliminate if general enterprise document search for internal employees is required (prefer Amazon Q Business).
use_when: Building AI-driven contact center solutions with real-time GenAI call transcription and agent assistance.
Automating customer service phone and chat interactions using natural language understanding.
avoid_when: Eliminate if general enterprise document search for internal employees is required (prefer Amazon Q Business).
Eliminate if standalone text speech-to-text translation is needed (prefer Transcribe).
items.standout_feature: Cloud contact center service enabling AI-powered agent assistance, interactive voice response (IVR), and speech analytics.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon Lex: Connect provides a complete contact center telephony/chat platform, whereas Lex is specifically the conversational NLU bot engine used within Connect.
vs Amazon Q Business: Connect focuses on customer contact center workflows, while Q Business focuses on internal workforce enterprise document search.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Cloud contact center; GenAI agent assist, call workflows, and transcripts.

items.name: Amazon Aurora
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Managed MySQL- and PostgreSQL-compatible relational database; choose Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector for relational/vector use cases.
items.boundary: Eliminate if unstructured log search or massive scale vector-first analytics is required (prefer OpenSearch).
use_when: Enterprise applications requiring high-throughput, low-latency relational ACID transactions.
Storing structured operational application data with built-in Pgvector extension for vector search capabilities.
avoid_when: Eliminate if unstructured log search or massive scale vector-first analytics is required (prefer OpenSearch).
Eliminate if simple key-value nosql storage with single-digit millisecond latency is requested (prefer DynamoDB).
items.standout_feature: High-performance relational database engine compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL, featuring auto-scaling storage and serverless options.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon RDS: Aurora is AWS's proprietary cloud-native database engine with multi-AZ shared storage replication, whereas standard RDS manages traditional database engines (Oracle, SQL Server, standard PostgreSQL).
vs Amazon DynamoDB: Aurora is a relational SQL database with ACID transactions, whereas DynamoDB is a NoSQL key-value store.
vs Amazon OpenSearch Service: Aurora is for structured OLTP transactions (with optional pgvector), while OpenSearch is dedicated to search indexing and log analytics.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Managed MySQL- and PostgreSQL-compatible relational database; choose Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector for relational/vector use cases.

items.name: Amazon DocumentDB

items.exam_role_and_boundary: Managed document database compatible with MongoDB workloads; not a default vector store.

use_when: Storing user session states, conversation history, or LLM agent memory state with high throughput and low latency.

vs Amazon ElastiCache: DynamoDB provides persistent primary NoSQL storage, whereas ElastiCache provides in-memory volatile caching.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Serverless key-value/document storage for sessions, job state, feedback, idempotency, memory metadata, TTL, and circuit breakers.

items.name: DynamoDB Streams

use_when: Triggering immediate downstream Lambda processing when DynamoDB table items are created or modified.
Replicating database changes in real time to search indexes or analytics systems.
avoid_when: Eliminate if continuous streaming of external application logs or telemetry is needed (prefer Kinesis).
Eliminate if rule-based event routing across AWS services is requested (prefer EventBridge).
items.standout_feature: Time-ordered log capturing real-time item-level modification events (insert, update, delete) in DynamoDB tables.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon Kinesis: DynamoDB Streams specifically captures item-level mutations inside a DynamoDB table, whereas Kinesis ingests general high-throughput data streams from external applications.
vs Amazon EventBridge: DynamoDB Streams is a dedicated change-data-capture stream for DynamoDB tables, whereas EventBridge is an enterprise event bus.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Change stream from DynamoDB for event-driven synchronization.

items.name: Bedrock Guardrails

Centralizing data lake security management and access auditing across analytics services.
avoid_when: Eliminate if executing ad-hoc SQL queries directly (prefer Athena).
Eliminate if building transactional database tables (prefer Aurora).
items.standout_feature: Central data lake management service for setting up, securing, and governing fine-grained column/row level access controls on S3 data lakes.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Glue: Lake Formation provides fine-grained row/column security governance over S3 data, whereas Glue provides the underlying catalog and ETL pipeline engine.
vs Amazon Athena: Lake Formation governs security permissions on S3 files, whereas Athena executes SQL queries against those files adhering to Lake Formation permissions.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Govern Data Catalog resources with table-, column-, row-, and cell-level permissions for integrated analytics paths. Not an FM content guardrail or a private network boundary.

items.name: Amazon Macie
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Discover/classify sensitive data stored in S3.
items.boundary: Eliminate if intercepting live Bedrock LLM input/output prompts in real time (prefer Bedrock Guardrails).
use_when: Automatically scanning Amazon S3 buckets to discover and alert on exposed personally identifiable information (PII) or credentials.
Auditing S3 bucket security postures for unencrypted or publicly accessible data.
avoid_when: Eliminate if intercepting live Bedrock LLM input/output prompts in real time (prefer Bedrock Guardrails).
Eliminate if performing general NLP text entity extraction via API (prefer Comprehend).
items.standout_feature: Data security service that uses machine learning and pattern matching to discover and protect sensitive PII data stored in Amazon S3.
distinction_notes: vs Bedrock Guardrails: Macie scans static S3 buckets at rest for PII, whereas Bedrock Guardrails filters real-time model prompts and responses.
vs Amazon Comprehend: Macie automatically inspects S3 storage for data security compliance, whereas Comprehend is an NLP API for text analysis.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Discover/classify sensitive data stored in S3.

items.name: AWS Secrets Manager
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Store, retrieve, and rotate secrets; scope tool roles to specific secrets.
items.boundary: Eliminate if deploying dynamic non-sensitive application feature flags and configuration files (prefer AppConfig).
use_when: Storing database credentials or third-party API keys with automated lifecycle rotation.
Eliminating hardcoded credentials from application source code and Lambda functions.
avoid_when: Eliminate if deploying dynamic non-sensitive application feature flags and configuration files (prefer AppConfig).
Eliminate if managing cryptographic master keys (prefer KMS).
items.standout_feature: Secrets management service for securely storing, retrieving, and automatically rotating database credentials, API keys, and OAuth tokens.
distinction_notes: vs AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store: Secrets Manager features automatic secret rotation and built-in cross-account sharing, whereas Parameter Store is a general parameter store without built-in rotation for standard tier.
vs AWS AppConfig: Secrets Manager manages confidential credentials, while AppConfig manages application runtime configuration rollouts.
vs AWS KMS: Secrets Manager stores secret strings (passwords, keys), while KMS manages the underlying encryption keys used to encrypt those secrets.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Store, retrieve, and rotate secrets; scope tool roles to specific secrets.

items.name: AWS WAF
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Web request filtering/rate rules at API/edge; not an FM semantic guardrail.
items.boundary: Eliminate if filtering LLM semantic prompt content, harmful topics, or hallucinations (prefer Bedrock Guardrails).
use_when: Protecting API Gateway or CloudFront endpoints against SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), and rate-based DDoS attacks.
Filtering HTTP web requests based on IP addresses, geographical locations, or HTTP header patterns.
avoid_when: Eliminate if filtering LLM semantic prompt content, harmful topics, or hallucinations (prefer Bedrock Guardrails).
Eliminate if managing internal resource IAM permissions.
items.standout_feature: Web application firewall that protects web applications and APIs against common web exploits (SQLi, XSS, rate-limiting) at layer 7.
distinction_notes: vs Bedrock Guardrails: WAF inspects network layer 7 web traffic (SQLi, XSS, rate limits), whereas Bedrock Guardrails inspects LLM semantic prompts and response content.
vs Amazon API Gateway: WAF is the security firewall filter attached to API Gateway endpoints to block malicious traffic.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Web request filtering/rate rules at API/edge; not an FM semantic guardrail.

items.name: Amazon EBS
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Block storage for EC2/SageMaker endpoint model artifacts and containers.
items.boundary: Eliminate if shared file system access across hundreds of concurrent compute instances is required (prefer EFS).
use_when: Attaching low-latency, high-IOPS persistent block storage to single EC2 instances for databases or local model caching.
Block storage requirements needing persistent root volume storage for EC2 virtual machines.
avoid_when: Eliminate if shared file system access across hundreds of concurrent compute instances is required (prefer EFS).
Eliminate if scalable object storage for documents is needed (prefer S3).
items.standout_feature: High-performance block storage volumes designed for persistent use with Amazon EC2 instances.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon EFS: EBS is a single-instance block volume attached to one EC2 instance, whereas EFS is a shared POSIX file system accessible by multiple instances concurrently.
vs Amazon S3: EBS provides block storage for EC2 file systems, whereas S3 provides REST-accessible object storage.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Block storage for EC2/SageMaker endpoint model artifacts and containers.

items.name: Amazon EFS
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Shared elastic file system for multiple compute clients.
items.boundary: Eliminate if single-instance dedicated high-IOPS block storage is needed (prefer EBS).
use_when: Providing a shared persistent file system accessible by hundreds of EC2 instances, containers, or Lambda functions concurrently.
Shared storage requirements for multi-container ML training or persistent container workspace directories.
avoid_when: Eliminate if single-instance dedicated high-IOPS block storage is needed (prefer EBS).
Eliminate if web-accessible object storage for unstructured media files is requested (prefer S3).
items.standout_feature: Fully managed, scalable POSIX-compliant shared file system for use with Amazon EC2, ECS, EKS, and AWS Lambda.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon EBS: EFS provides shared POSIX file access across multiple instances simultaneously, whereas EBS attaches to a single EC2 instance at a time.
vs Amazon S3: EFS is a hierarchical POSIX file system mounted by compute tasks, whereas S3 is an object store accessed via HTTP/REST APIs.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Shared elastic file system for multiple compute clients.

items.name: Amazon S3
items.importance_reason: Implementation-depth service used directly in production design and troubleshooting decisions.
items.role: Source documents, datasets, artifacts, batch input/output, logs, and durable object storage.
items.boundary: Eliminate if POSIX file system mounting or low-latency block volume storage is required (prefer EFS or EBS).
use_when: Storing unstructured training datasets, document files, model checkpoints, and evaluation results.
Primary storage foundation for RAG knowledge bases, data lakes, and backup artifacts.
avoid_when: Eliminate if POSIX file system mounting or low-latency block volume storage is required (prefer EFS or EBS).
Eliminate if relational ACID database queries are needed (prefer Aurora).
items.standout_feature: Industry-leading object storage service offering unmatched durability, scalability, security, and performance for data lakes and AI artifacts.
distinction_notes: vs Amazon EBS: S3 is web-accessible object storage, whereas EBS is single-instance block storage attached to EC2.
vs Amazon EFS: S3 is object storage accessed via APIs, whereas EFS is a shared file system mounted by OS instances.
vs Amazon DynamoDB: S3 stores large binary objects and files, whereas DynamoDB stores structured key-value database records.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Source documents, datasets, artifacts, batch input/output, logs, and durable object storage.

items.name: S3 Intelligent-Tiering
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Automatic storage-cost tiering for changing access patterns.
items.boundary: Eliminate if access patterns are strictly known in advance for time-based retention (prefer standard S3 Lifecycle rules).
use_when: Optimizing storage costs for datasets with unknown, changing, or unpredictable access patterns automatically.
Automatic cost optimization for long-term document or model checkpoint archives without retrieval fees.
avoid_when: Eliminate if access patterns are strictly known in advance for time-based retention (prefer standard S3 Lifecycle rules).
Eliminate if cross-region data replication is requested (prefer Cross-Region Replication).
items.standout_feature: Automated S3 storage class that optimizes storage costs by dynamically moving objects between access tiers without operational overhead.
distinction_notes: vs S3 Lifecycle: S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically monitors and moves objects based on UNKNOWN access patterns, whereas S3 Lifecycle rules move objects based on KNOWN fixed schedule rules (e.g. 30 days).
vs S3 Cross-Region Replication: Intelligent-Tiering manages cost tiers within buckets, while Cross-Region Replication copies objects to different geographic regions.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Automatic storage-cost tiering for changing access patterns.

items.name: S3 Lifecycle
items.importance_reason: Architecture-decision service that must be distinguished from nearby alternatives.
items.role: Retention, transition, and expiry for prompts, responses, and audit data.
items.boundary: Eliminate if data access patterns are completely unknown and dynamic (prefer S3 Intelligent-Tiering).
use_when: Automating object transitions to Glacier or expiring temporary log files after a fixed number of days.
Enforcing predictable data retention schedules and deletion schedules for compliance.
avoid_when: Eliminate if data access patterns are completely unknown and dynamic (prefer S3 Intelligent-Tiering).
Eliminate if geographic disaster recovery replication is needed (prefer Cross-Region Replication).
items.standout_feature: Rule-based automation engine for managing S3 objects throughout their lifecycle, including tier transitions and expiration.
distinction_notes: vs S3 Intelligent-Tiering: S3 Lifecycle requires setting explicit fixed age-based transition rules, whereas Intelligent-Tiering dynamically moves objects based on observed access patterns.
vs S3 Cross-Region Replication: S3 Lifecycle handles object transitions/deletions, whereas Cross-Region Replication handles geographic data copying.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Retention, transition, and expiry for prompts, responses, and audit data.

items.name: S3 Cross-Region Replication
items.importance_reason: Recognition-depth service: know its role and eliminate it when scenario constraints do not match.
items.role: Replicate objects for resilience/compliance when destination residency is allowed.
items.boundary: Eliminate if moving data within the same region between storage classes (prefer S3 Lifecycle).
use_when: Replicating critical GenAI datasets or model artifacts to a secondary AWS Region for disaster recovery (DR).
Meeting strict multi-region data compliance and low-latency local access requirements.
avoid_when: Eliminate if moving data within the same region between storage classes (prefer S3 Lifecycle).
Eliminate if migrating on-premises files to S3 over network (prefer DataSync).
items.standout_feature: Automated mechanism for asynchronously copying S3 objects across buckets in different AWS Regions for disaster recovery and latency.
distinction_notes: vs S3 Lifecycle: Cross-Region Replication copies data across geographic Regions for DR, whereas Lifecycle manages storage class transitions within buckets.
vs AWS DataSync: Cross-Region Replication is built-in S3-to-S3 bucket replication, whereas DataSync migrates data between external on-premises storage and AWS.
items.exam_role_and_boundary: Replicate objects for resilience/compliance when destination residency is allowed.
